posts.content:
We're hiring at GreyLabs AI - Senior Product Manager (Mumbai / Bengaluru) 🚀 

Few months ago, we closed our Series A (~₹100 Cr), and we're doubling down on what got us here: building Voice AI that actually works for India's biggest banks, NBFCs, fintechs, and insurance companies.

50+ enterprise customers. Millions of conversations. Collections, sales, servicing, compliance - real workflows, real scale, real complexity.

We need a Senior Product Manager who can own a charter end-to-end and help us define what this platform becomes next.

Here's who we're looking for:
✅  Someone with genuine depth - either in BFSI (you know how a bank actually operates, not just what the org chart says) or in Voice AI (you've shipped conversational AI products and watched them break at scale). You bring one, you'll pick up the other fast.
✅  Someone who runs discovery with CXOs in the morning and unblocks engineering in the afternoon. Who knows that a feature isn't "shipped" until it's embedded in a customer's workflow and creating measurable value.
✅  Someone who thrives in ambiguity. Voice AI in Indian financial services is not a solved category - the use cases, the regulation, the customer expectations are still being written. That should excite you, not intimidate you.

6+ years in product management. B2B/enterprise DNA. Strong opinions, loosely held. Clear communication across every altitude - boardroom to backend. If you've been looking for a role where ownership is real, the problem space is massive, and the team moves fast with high craft standards, let's talk!

Please find the application link in my comment below 🔗 

#hiring #productmanagement #voiceai #fintech #startups #jobalert | 17 comments on LinkedIn
posts.summary:
We're hiring at GreyLabs AI - Senior Product Manager (Mumbai / Bengaluru) 🚀 

Few months ago, we closed our Series A (~₹100 Cr), and we're doubling down on what got us here: building Voice AI that actually works for India's biggest banks, NBFCs, fintechs, and insurance companies. 50+ enterprise customers. Millions of conversations.

posts.content:
I’ve been spending a lot of time thinking about one question:
Why does Indian fintech feel so different from the rest of the world?

After working across cross-border fund flows, regulations, and product structures, one thing is clear to me:
This is not just a difference in markets.
It’s a difference in philosophy.

India built fintech on public infrastructure.
The world built fintech on private innovation.

And that single choice has changed everything.
In India:
Payments scaled because policy enabled it (not because it was profitable)
Interoperability is mandatory—not optional
Compliance shapes product design from day one
Globally:
Payments still make money
Innovation happens on top of legacy rails

Regulation often follows innovation—not the other way around
But here’s where it gets interesting…
We’re now seeing a convergence.
India is moving from:
Scale → Monetisation → Sustainability
Global markets are moving from:
Innovation → Regulation → Discipline

And both are meeting at the same place:

Compliance is no longer a cost centre. It’s becoming a strategic advantage.
The winners in the next decade won’t be the fastest builders.

They’ll be the ones who can:
Design clean, regulator-aligned fund flows
Operate across multiple jurisdictions seamlessly
Build compliance into the architecture—not as an afterthought

Because here’s the hard truth I’ve learned firsthand:
Fintech doesn’t break because of lack of innovation.
It breaks when innovation outruns regulatory understanding.

India got one thing right early—maybe painfully so:
Control before chaos
Structure before scale
And now, as global fintech matures, the world is slowly rediscovering the same principle.

My biggest takeaway?
The future of fintech won’t be built by choosing between India or the West.
It will be built by combining both.
Build with the discipline of India.
Innovate with the ambition of global markets.

Because in the end:
Trust is not a feature.
It is the product.

#fintech #scale #regulation #compliance #indiavsglobal
posts.summary:
I’ve been spending a lot of time thinking about one question:
Why does Indian fintech feel so different from the rest of the world? After working across cross-border fund flows, regulations, and product structures, one thing is clear to me:
This is not just a difference in markets. It’s a difference in philosophy.

posts.content:
Shri Rajesh Kumar Agarwal Appointed as Director (Finance) of PFC

The Government of India has appointed Shri Rajesh Kumar Agarwal as Director (Finance) at Power Finance Corporation Ltd. (PFC), effective 23rd April 2026. He was previously serving as Executive Director (Finance) at PFC.

With over 31 years of experience in the power and financial sectors, he has been associated with PFC since 2009. He has contributed in key areas such as corporate accounts, fund management, lending, and financial reporting, while also leading digital reforms.

Before joining PFC, he worked with NTPC, NPCIL, and DTL in core finance and policy-related roles. | 14 comments on LinkedIn
posts.summary:

With the market getting ready for public issues, there is a need for a regulatory framework for RIghts issues, Preferential issues and Qualified… | Pradeep Ramakrishnan
posts.content:
IFSCA Official believes in getting future ready. 

With the market getting ready for public issues, there is a need for a regulatory framework for RIghts issues, Preferential issues and Qualified Institutions Placement. Accordingly, IFSCA has, yesterday, issued circulars for companies listed on the IFSC for these further alterations of capital structure by issuers at the IFSC.

The circulars can be found here:

Preferential allotment and Qualified Institutions Placement:

https://lnkd.in/g7NXF64H

Rights issues:

https://lnkd.in/gJK9HtPr

Arjun Prasad
Shubham Goyal
NSE International Exchange (NSEIX)
India International Exchange (IFSC) Ltd (India INX)
posts.summary: IFSCA Official believes in getting future ready.
